policy_statement: Line of Action #7: Reduce or minimise risks associated with flooding through flood risk management planning and measures such as infiltration areas, river profile restoration, protective infrastructure, monitoring/modelling, warning systems, and stormwater drainage upgrades.
mechanism_description: Implements flood risk management plans and measures (infiltration areas, drainage upgrades, monitoring/modelling, and forecasting/warning systems) to reduce river and urban flood risk and improve infrastructure resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme rainfall/riverine flooding) → ↓ exposure & vulnerability (via protection, drainage capacity, infiltration, warning) → ↓ flood damages & service disruption
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes to the flood-risk management system: land/watershed interventions (infiltration areas, river profile restoration), stormwater/drainage upgrades, protective works, and operational systems (monitoring, modelling, forecasting, warning/response). (2) These measures mediate impacts by increasing conveyance/storage/infiltration, reducing peak runoff and water levels, preventing overtopping/backflow, and improving lead time for protective actions. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is reduced flood risk (lower probability and/or severity of inundation), improved resilience of critical infrastructure, and reduced losses during climate-driven extreme precipitation and flood events.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing exposure and vulnerability to river and urban flooding (and associated infrastructure/service disruption) under current and future climate extremes.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: managing a climate hazard (flooding) to reduce exposure/vulnerability and damages—an adaptation outcome.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while some measures (e.g., infiltration/green infrastructure) can have co-benefits (urban cooling, carbon, water quality), the central, largest climate effect across all such policies is risk reduction from flooding and improved infrastructure resilience, not emissions mitigation or energy/material efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a specific policy instance were solely framed/implemented for water efficiency or ecosystem carbon sequestration, classification could differ, but the stated mechanism explicitly targets flood risk reduction and resilience, keeping Adaptation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any policy whose core causal chain is flood risk management measures → reduced flood risk/resilient infrastructure follows the same adaptation pathway regardless of whether measures are grey (drainage upgrades) or green (infiltration/retention).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., grey vs green infrastructure, early warning vs structural protection) is deferred to policy-level evidence and does not change the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Green infiltration/retention elements can overlap with Nature-Based Solutions, but under this canonical mechanism the dominant intended and climate-relevant outcome is flood-risk reduction/resilience (Adaptation); mitigation co-benefits (e.g., avoided repair emissions) are typically minor and not dominant.
key_indicators: “Implementing Flood Risk Management Plans”; “Reduce the risk of river and urban flooding”; “Creating infiltration areas”; “Implementation of forecasting and warning systems”; “Remodelling of urban stormwater drainage networks”; “Increasing the resilience of infrastructure”
co_benefits: Potential water quality improvements and groundwater recharge from infiltration areas; urban amenity/ecosystem benefits from river restoration; general disaster-risk reduction benefits beyond climate-attributed events.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes multiple instrument modalities (planning plus infrastructure upgrades and warning systems). Classified by the dominant framing as a plan/strategy (“Flood Risk Management Plans”) rather than as a specific capital investment program.
